title: Cloudflare Rate Limiting Rule Trigger Volume (`cloudflare:json`)
dataSources: Cloudflare Logpush JSON delivered to Splunk HEC/S3 with `sourcetype=cloudflare:json` (HTTP requests, firewall, Workers, DNS, Access as configured)
app: Cloudflare App for Splunk (Splunkbase 4501) + Logpush JSON (`cloudflare:json`)
spl:
index=waf sourcetype="cloudflare:json" earliest=-24h
| spath RuleID
| spath ClientIP
| where match(_raw,"(?i)ratelimit|rate.limit|RL:")
| stats count by RuleID, ClientIP, EdgeColoCode
| sort -count
description: Rate limits are cost controls and abuse brakes. Sudden triggers may mean a partner changed behavior or an attack started.
value: Operations and security teams track when rate-limiting rules fire, identify whether triggers come from legitimate partners exceeding quotas or from distributed abuse, and tune thresholds to balance availability with protection.
implementation: Configure Logpush jobs per dataset; route all into a unified `cloudflare:json` sourcetype or use transforms to normalize. Use `spath` for PascalCase fields. Create macros per zone name.
cimSpl:
| tstats `summariesonly` count
  from datamodel=Web.Web
  by Web.user, Web.url, Web.http_method span=1h
| sort - count
knownFalsePositives: Anomalies from new SaaS app rollouts, browser updates, or vendor URL category changes.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Cloudflare App for Splunk (Splunkbase 4501) v2.x on the Search Head. Logpush configured for the **Firewall Events** dataset with fields: `RuleID`, `Action`, `Source`, `ClientIP`, `ClientRequestHost`, `ClientRequestURI`, `RayID`.
• Rate Limiting rules configured in Cloudflare (available on Pro+ plans for basic rules, Enterprise for Advanced Rate Limiting). Each rule has a unique `RuleID` visible in the firewall events when `Source=rateLimit`.
• Data routed to `index=waf` with `sourcetype=cloudflare:json`.
• Create a `cf_rate_limit_rules.csv` lookup: `rule_id,rule_name,threshold,window_seconds,target_path,owner` for mapping rule IDs to human-readable names and context.

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Rate limiting events appear in the Firewall Events Logpush dataset with `Source=rateLimit`. Verify:
```spl
index=waf sourcetype="cloudflare:json" earliest=-24h
| spath Source | where Source="rateLimit"
| spath RuleID | spath Action
| stats count by RuleID, Action
```
You should see actions like `block`, `challenge`, `log` for each rate-limiting rule. If empty but you have rate-limit rules configured: the rules may not have triggered in the last 24h (good), or the Logpush job may not include the Firewall Events dataset.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert

Primary search — Rate limit triggers by rule and source IP:
```spl
index=waf sourcetype="cloudflare:json" earliest=-24h
| spath Source | where Source="rateLimit"
| spath RuleID | spath Action | spath ClientIP | spath ClientRequestHost
| stats count as triggers dc(ClientIP) as unique_ips values(ClientRequestHost) as zones earliest(_time) as first_seen latest(_time) as last_seen by RuleID, Action
| eval duration_min=round((last_seen - first_seen)/60, 0)
| lookup cf_rate_limit_rules.csv rule_id as RuleID OUTPUT rule_name threshold
| sort - triggers
```

Understanding this SPL: Groups rate-limit triggers by rule and action type. High `unique_ips` with the same rule means a broad distributed attack — DDoS or credential stuffing from a botnet. Low `unique_ips` with high triggers means a single aggressive client — often a misconfigured partner API or a scraper. The `duration_min` field shows whether the abuse is sustained or bursty.

Trending:
```spl
index=waf sourcetype="cloudflare:json" earliest=-7d
| spath Source | where Source="rateLimit"
| spath RuleID | spath Action | where Action="block"
| lookup cf_rate_limit_rules.csv rule_id as RuleID OUTPUT rule_name
| eval label=if(isnotnull(rule_name), rule_name, RuleID)
| timechart span=1h count by label limit=10 useother=f
```

Step 3 — Validate
(a) In Cloudflare: Security → Events → filter Source = Rate Limiting, last 24h. Compare rule trigger counts with Splunk.
(b) Generate test traffic: use `curl` in a loop against a rate-limited endpoint to deliberately trigger the rule. Confirm the events appear in Splunk within the Logpush delivery interval (typically 30-60 seconds).
(c) Verify the `cf_rate_limit_rules.csv` lookup maps correctly by spot-checking 3 rule IDs against the Cloudflare dashboard → Security → WAF → Rate Limiting Rules.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Dashboard ("Cloudflare — Rate Limiting"):
• Row 1 — Single-value tiles: "Total rate-limit blocks (24h)", "Rules triggered", "Unique IPs blocked".
• Row 2 — Timechart: triggers per rule over 7 days.
• Row 3 — Table: rule name, threshold, trigger count, unique IPs, top zone, duration.

Alert: Trigger when any single rule exceeds 10x its 7-day hourly average in a 1h window. This catches sudden abuse spikes while ignoring steady background noise.

Runbook:
1. **Partner API triggering rate limits** — The partner may have increased their polling frequency. Check `ClientIP` against known partner IPs. If legitimate: adjust the rate-limit threshold or add the partner to an IP Access Rule allowlist.
2. **Distributed attack across many IPs** — Rate limiting alone may be insufficient. Escalate to Cloudflare's DDoS mitigation team or enable Bot Fight Mode on the affected zone.
3. **Rate limit rule never triggers** — Either traffic hasn't reached the threshold, or the rule is misconfigured. Check the rule's match expression and threshold in the Cloudflare dashboard.

Step 5 — Troubleshooting
• **No rate-limit events in Splunk** — Firewall Events Logpush job may not be configured, or no rate-limit rules have triggered. Verify: `index=waf sourcetype="cloudflare:json" | stats count by Source` to see what event sources are arriving.
• **RuleID doesn't match any configured rule** — Cloudflare may have deprecated or renumbered the rule. Check the Cloudflare API: `GET /zones/{zone_id}/ratelimits`.
• **Events show `Action=log` but no `Action=block`** — The rate-limit rule is in log-only mode (common during initial deployment). Change to block mode in the Cloudflare dashboard when ready.
grandmaExplanation: We watch our speed limits on the website — when too many requests come from the same place too fast, we see who is being blocked and whether the limits need adjusting.
